coredata.dc:title: Innovative linkage sensor based on bimodal excitation behavior nitrogen doped carbon dots for ratiometric and visual detection of fluoride ion in food
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Guoqiang
Li, Tingting
Wang, Yiru
Li, Lintao
Tang, Siyuan
Hu, Houwen
Jiang, Hai
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Fluoride ion (F−), particularly an important substance for growth, development and bone metabolism, is one of the essential micronutrients in living organisms. Therefore, F− detection becomes one of the main problems that need to be addressed in ordinary living. Herein, an innovative bimodal excitation-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for ratiometric detection of fluoride ion (F−) is developed by multistage response to curcumin and F− based on the localization of inner filter effect (L-IFE). Primarily, curcumin decreases the N-CDs fluorescence due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Subsequently, the cascade addition of F− changes the absorption band from 526 to 418 nm due to the generation of curcumin-F− complex. Thus, the L-IFE occurs, and uneven overlap between the curcumin-F− complex absorption and N-CDs excitation leads to the inverse variation in fluorescence signals under the illumination of 399 nm and 470 nm. This multistage sensing system exhibits excellent sensitivity toward F−, with a linear range from 0 to 50 µM and a low detection limit of 57 nM. Moreover, a smart-integrated sensing platform is designed for visual F− detection in practical samples. Thus, our work provides a novel guidance for F− detection in food environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimodal excitation ratiometric sensing
Linkage sensor
The localized inner filter effect
Fluoride ion
Smart-integrated sensing system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: F− detection using the N-CDs/curcumin ratiometric sensing system
Analysis of F− content in actual water samples
Visual detection of F− based on smartphone-integrated detection system
Fluorescence properties and stability of bimodal excitation N-CDs
Feasibility of N-CDs/curcumin system for F− detection based on bimodal excitation-based ratiometric method
The optimized sensing conditions of F− determination
Bimodal excitation-based ratiometric sensing of F−
Mechanism of the N-CDs/curcumin system response to F−
Smartphone-integrated sensing platform for visual and real-time determination of F−

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and surface structure of N-CDs. (a) TEM image, diameter distribution (the inserted figure in top left corner) and HR-TEM image (the inserted figure in bottom right corner) of N-CDs. (b) FT-IR spectrum, (c) XPS spectrum of N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s and (f) O 1s.

Optical features of N-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of N-CDs. Insets: the images of N-CDs solution under daylight (left) and UV light at 365 nm (right). (b) PL spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Excitation-emission matrix graph map for N-CDs. (d) 13C NMR spectrum of N-CDs. (e) 1H NMR spectrum of N-CDs. (f) Summary of emission wavelengths and QYs of CDs obtained in this work and previous reports. Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs under different ultraviolet irradiation timescales (g), different visible light irradiation timescales (h) and different concentration of NaCl aqueous solutions (i).

(a) PLE spectra of N-CDs with gradually increased concentration of F−. (b) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (I 470/I 399) and the concentration of F−. (c) Linear fitting between I 470/I 399 and the concentration of F− within a wide range from 0 to 50 μM. The 3D fluorescence spectra and spectral overlapping of the UV–vis absorption spectrum: (d) N-CDs, (e) N-CDs/curcumin and (f) N-CDs/curcumin-F−. (g) The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, curcumin-F−, N-CDs/curcumin-F− and N-CDs + curcumin-F−. (h) Lifetime decay curves of N-CDs, N-CDs/curcumin at 559 nm emission under the excitations of 399 nm and 470 nm. (i) Lifetime decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs/curcumin-F− at 559 nm emission under the excitations of 399 nm and 470 nm. (j) Schematic diagram of the response mechanism of the linkage system for detecting F−.

(a) Flow diagram of smartphone-assisted sensing platform for F− detection. (b) Fluorescence photographs of the N-CDs/curcumin system with various concentrations of F− under excitations of 365 nm and 470 nm. (c) The relationship between S2/S1 ratio values and the concentration of F−. (d) Fluorescence photographs of the bimodal excitation-based sensing system after quantitative addition of actual samples spiked F− (50 µM).
Schematic illustration of the one-step synthesis of N-CDs, the construction process of the sensing platform and the application for the detection of F− based on smart-integrated RGB color analysis.

head.ce:title: Innovative linkage sensor based on bimodal excitation behavior nitrogen doped carbon dots for ratiometric and visual detection of fluoride ion in food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG)
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluoride ion (F−), particularly an important substance for growth, development and bone metabolism, is one of the essential micronutrients in living organisms. Therefore, F− detection becomes one of the main problems that need to be addressed in ordinary living. Herein, an innovative bimodal excitation-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for ratiometric detection of fluoride ion (F−) is developed by multistage response to curcumin and F− based on the localization of inner filter effect (L-IFE). Primarily, curcumin decreases the N-CDs fluorescence due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Subsequently, the cascade addition of F− changes the absorption band from 526 to 418 nm due to the generation of curcumin-F− complex. Thus, the L-IFE occurs, and uneven overlap between the curcumin-F− complex absorption and N-CDs excitation leads to the inverse variation in fluorescence signals under the illumination of 399 nm and 470 nm. This multistage sensing system exhibits excellent sensitivity toward F−, with a linear range from 0 to 50 µM and a low detection limit of 57 nM. Moreover, a smart-integrated sensing platform is designed for visual F− detection in practical samples. Thus, our work provides a novel guidance for F− detection in food environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lately, accumulating evidences from news and studies suggest that the excessive addition of food additives and halide ions to food and domestic water for commercial profit has been gradually exposed to the public [1]. Problems associated with food safety may harm public health and even the stability of social and economic development. Even now, food safety has remained to be resolved in society, and it is increasingly important to strengthen relevant regulations. Fluoride is one of the pre-requisite minor elements for the human body, but the quantities of fluoride ion (F−) and fluoride polluted water is rapidly increasing in the wake of human activities and industrial growth [2,3]. Dietary intake of fluoride is required for preventing dental caries. However, human poisoning will be inevitably caused by excessive intake of F− due to its slow excretion from the body. At the same time, some diseases will occur, such as nephrolithiasis and kidney failure, osteoporosis, tooth and skeletal fluorosis, lesions of organs and inhibition of neurotransmitter biosynthesis in foetuses [4,5]. Of the statement from the World Health Organization (WHO) that an acceptable maximum standard of F− detected from drinking water is 1 mg‧L−1 (52.6 µM), hence quantitative F− detection is extremely urgent in the fields of food environment.

Currently, many strategies have been designed to detect F−, including colorimetry,[6] mass spectroscopy,[7] nuclear magnetic resonance,[8] inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES),[9] high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methods [10]. Unfortunately, these treatment methods generally have the characteristics of expensive and bulky instruments, time-consuming detection process, complicated sample pretreatment or electrode modifications and lack of multifunctionality, limiting their widespread use. Intriguingly, fluorometry, as a powerful analytical technique, has become a very meaningful research topic in F− sensing. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs), as a new rising star in the carbon family, have been known for their low cost, easy preparation and superior optical properties, and provide significant applications in many fields such as fluorescence sensing. To date, some researchers have achieved sensitive detection of F− by constructing CDs-based sensors. For example, Raveendran et al. produced arrowroot-derived CDs for sensing of F− and obtained a limit of detection (LOD) of 521 nM [11]. Che et al. designed a multistage fluorescence sensing system to detect F− by fluorescence variation from quenching to recovering response to the presence of Eu3+ and F− [12]. Li et al. engineered a multi-signals fluorescence sensor by immobilizing the pyrene-boronic acid moieties with CDs for F− monitoring [13]. Nevertheless, among many fluorescence sensors, on the one hand, mono emission fluorescence probes are easily interfered by the detection environment and background. However, on the other hand, multi-signals probes constructed by tedious processes, such as the grafting and removal of surface modification, are restricted with the pairing of appropriate fluorophore and absorber. In comparison, the construction of multiple signals fluorescent sensors based on inner filter effect (IFE) can not only significantly enhance the color and brightness contrast but also provide more simplicity and flexibility because of no need for covalent linking of CDs with quencher or surface modification of CDs [14]. Moreover, much significant fluorescence information, such as light scattering, fluorescence polarization and excitation signals, are usually neglected, which similarly can be developed for ratiometric sensing [15]. The diversity of fluorescence sensors based on excitation signals is of significance and inspiration to the construction of ratiometric sensors. Furthermore, the sensors performed in the laboratory still have specific limitations, such as expensive instrumentation, skilled labor, complex sample pretreatment and time-consuming. In contrast, the smartphone-assisted visual sensing system can overcome the above problems and make the detection more intelligent, portable and miniaturized [16]. Therefore, constructing IFE-based excitation ratiometric fluorescence sensing device is still highly desperate and challenging for sensitive and visual sensing of F−.

In this study, the novel bimodal excitation N-CDs are synthesized solvothermally using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and melamine with an unexpectedly high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 27.8% (Scheme 1 ). Through coupling N-CDs with curcumin, a novel linkage excitation ratiometric fluorescence sensor is developed for detecting F− in concentration range of 0–50 µM with a LOD of 57 nM. Firstly, curcumin enables to weaken the fluorescence of N-CDs because of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). When adding F− continuously, the formation of curcumin-F− coordination compound brings about a notable blue-shift in the absorbance, and the fluorescence of N-CDs recovers at an excitation of 470 nm because of the localization of inner filter effect (L-IFE), whereas the N-CDs fluorescence remains the weakness state under 399 nm excitation. Furthermore, on the basis of the abovementioned sensing system, a smartphone-integrated sensing device is developed for F− determination on spot via applying RGB (Red, Green and Blue) values analysis in several water samples. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time to apply an excitation-based ratiometric sensing system in F− detection. Our strategy presents satisfying results with the relative recovery ranging from 102.64% to 103.72%. Therefore, this work presents an innovative thought for the facile design of IFE-based excitation ratiometric linkage sensor, which is used for portable and visual detection of F− in the food environment.

OPD (99.5%), melamine (99%), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.5%), curcumin (98%), acetone (≥99.7%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.99%), sodium fluoride (NaF, 99.99%), all cationic and anionic solutions and other substances were purchased from Aladdin. Deionized water (>18.2 MΩ‧cm) throughout all experiments was obtained from a Milli-R04 purification system. All chemical reagents were used as analytical grade without further purification.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of N-CDs were obtained by a Hitachi H-8100. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was gained using an atomic force microscope (Oxford Instruments, Cypher S). Powder X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was analyzed using the SHIMADZU 7000 X-ray Diffractometer (XRD) (Beijing, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) studies were performed on a PHI1580EXCA photoelectron spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were scanned by the PerkinElmer spectrum. Fluorescence measurements (Photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra) were made in solution using a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–vis) spectra were recorded by a Lambda900 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The 13C Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and 1H NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker Avance III 600 spectrometer. The fluorescence lifetimes were detected on Edinburgh FLS 1000 spectrometer.

The N-CDs were prepared by the solvothermal method. Primarily, 0.108 g of OPD and 0.101 g of melamine were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF and then sonicated for 8 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution was placed in a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. After maintaining at 150 °C for 8 h, it was cooled to atmospheric temperature. For purification of N-CDs, the residue was removed from the obtained mixture through a 0.22 μm filter. Finally, the purified substance was evaporated in a vacuum for 24 h and then stored at 4 °C before use.

Th QY of N-CDs was calculated according to an established procedure. In the related method, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (literature QY of 0.54) was chosen as the standard reference. The QY of N-CDs was calculated as follows (1):[17]
(1)
φ x
=
φ s
×
Grad
x
/
Grad
s
×
η x
/
η s
2
where subscripts × and s are the representations of N-CDs and a standard substance, respectively. The φ, η and Grad are referred to as the QY, refractive index of solvent and the slope corresponding to the integral of fluorescence area and absorbance value. In the aqueous solution, ηx /ηs ≈1.

The working solution was prepared by dissolving the as-prepared N-CDs in 20 mL of buffer solution (pH = 9). The pH level of buffers was adjusted by the NaOH (1 M) or HCl (1 M) solutions. Then, 1.5 mL of the obtained N-CDs solution (2 mg‧mL−1) and curcumin solution (3.6 mM; 50 μL) were added to a quartz cuvette, followed by diluting the mixture to 3.0 mL with pH = 9 buffer solution. After incubating for 3 min at room temperature, the PLE spectra were monitored at PL = 559 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: F− detection using the N-CDs/curcumin ratiometric sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: For detecting F−, the N-CDs/curcumin solution (3.0 mL) and F− (50 μL, different concentrations) were added to microtubes. Followed by incubation for 180 s, the PLE spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded at PL = 559 nm, and fluorescence intensities at 399 nm and 470 nm were measured for quantitative detection. All the experiments were conducted thrice, and the means of the results were used in the subsequent analysis.

The selectivity of the assay was investigated through screening its response to several competing ions (Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, K+, Al3+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Na+, Ba2+, Sn2+, ClO4 −, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, SO4 2− and CO3 2−). Then these corresponding fluorescence intensities were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of F− content in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: All environmental water samples were gained in the urban area of Ningbo, Zhejiang Province, and the milk was purchased at the local supermarket. Subsequently, all the samples were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 4 min before analysis, followed by filtering through 0.22 μm filters for future use. The supernatant was diluted to an appropriate concentration with deionized water. Then, the F− content of each sample was determined by adding F− (0 µM, 15 µM, 60 µM and 80 µM) for testing the applicability of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of F− based on smartphone-integrated detection system
ce:section.ce:para:
To achieve a portable detection system for F−, an N-CDs-based linkage sensor was integrated with a smartphone. Firstly, the F− of different concentrations was added in N-CDs/curcumin solutions (pH = 9) in the sample tank. After vigorously shaking to ensure homogenization, put it into the sample tank groove of the dark box equipment. Images were captured under two different excitation lamps (UV lamp and blue laser lamp) using the smartphone’s built-in camera at high resolution. Then the image of the sample tanks was saved to a file. Finally, the RGB value in each sample tank was determined by image analysis. Finally, the measured color intensity was reported in the color picker software.
(2)
Sum
R G B
v a l u e s = R e d + G r e e n + B l u e

The dispersity and morphology of N-CDs are analyzed by the TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), N-CDs are nearly spherical and uniformly dispersed with an average diameter of 3.6 nm. In the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (inset of Fig. 1(a)), the lattice spacing of N-CDs is 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite [18]. The XRD pattern of N-CDs shows a diffraction peak (2θ) of 22.1° (Figure S1), which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane [19]. These results are highly correlated, revealing that N-CDs have nanocrystalline cores of graphitic structure along with structural defects of sp3 hybridized carbons.

The characteristic absorption bands of surface functional groups of N-CDs are investigated by FT-IR analysis and represented in Fig. 1(b). As observed, many absorption peaks at 1000–1250 cm−1 (C-O), 1498 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 (C=O) and 3123–3500 cm−1 (O-H/N-H) regions, belong to the hydrophilic groups on the surface of N-CDs, which enable N-CDs to dispense well in aqueous solution. Some peaks at 1384 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1 regions are related to the stretching vibrations of C-N and C=C/C=N bonds, respectively, revealing the existence of polyaromatic structures within the N-CDs surface [20]. By comparing the FT-IR spectra of the reaction precursors OPD (purple line) and melamine (green line) in Figure S2, two strong peaks at approximately 3300 cm−1 region (–NH2 stretching vibration of OPD) disappear, and a new peak at 1498 cm−1 (C=O stretching vibration) occurs in N-CDs (yellow line), indicating that N-CDs are successfully doped with nitrogen and inherit the natural properties of raw materials [21,22]. Furthermore, XPS analysis are used to confirm the chemical state and elemental composition of N-CDs. Fig. 1(c) displays the full XPS spectrum of N-CDs, indicating that N-CDs mainly contain carbon (53.9%), nitrogen (36.6%) and oxygen (9.5%), corresponding to three typical binding energy peaks at 285.0 eV, 399.0 eV and 532.0 eV, respectively. As shown in the high-resolution C 1s spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 1(d)), three constituent binding energy peaks at 284.79 eV, 285.86 eV and 288.30 eV are ascribed to C-C, C-N/C-O and C=O structures of N-CDs, respectively [23,24]. For N 1s spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 1(e)), two constituent peaks correspond to C=N (399.05 eV) and C-N (399.98 eV) [25,26]. The two-constituent peaks of O 1s band (Fig. 1(f)) at 531.90 eV and 533.45 eV belong to C=O and C-O, respectively [27,28]. The analysis results of XPS and FT-IR are nearly consistent each other, suggesting that N-CDs are decorated by abundant oxygen/nitrogen-containing function groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and stability of bimodal excitation N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the fluorescence properties of N-CDs, the UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra are studied. As presented in Fig. 2 (a), UV–vis absorption spectrum (purple line) shows that one clear pinnacle at 278 nm is ascribed to the π-π* transition of C=C. In addition, it can be seen in the lower-energy region that a soft and broad peak at 425 nm corresponding to the n-π* transition of the surface groups [29]. The inset displays that the aqueous with dispensed N-CDs appears a transparent yellow under visible light, and exhibits a bright yellow fluorescence under UV irradiation. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra suggest that N-CDs perform an excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior resulting from the homogeneous surface structure (Fig. 2(b)) [30]. The excitation-emission matrix of N-CDs (Fig. 2(c)) displays two large areas, corresponding to the bimodal excitation phenomenon in Fig. 2(a). Furthermore, the connectivity and chemical structures of the N-CDs are characterized by 1H (1H NMR) and 13C (13C NMR) nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopic studies in D2O. The 13C NMR of N-CDs (Fig. 2(d)) shows some strong signals located in 30–40 ppm (for sp3 carbons and carbons attached with hydroxyl groups), 100–120 ppm (for C=C aromatic or sp2 carbons) and 160–175 ppm (for carbonyl carbons) [31,32]. Besides, in the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 2(e)), the signals coming from hydroxyl and ether groups are founded at 4.5–5.8 ppm. The strong signals in 6.9–7.6 ppm and 8–10 ppm belong to sp2 hydrogen or aromatic and aldehydic protons, respectively [33]. Taken the FT-IR, XPS and NMR data together, it is concluded that N-CDs contain nitrogen dopants and are abundant in oxygen/nitrogen-containing surface functional groups including amine, carbonyl and hydroxyl, which is associated closely with the structural characteristics and PLE properties of N-CDs [26]. Specifically, the introduction of N and O atoms may create new energy levels and change electric potential in N-CDs, resulting in distinct bimodal excitation states on N-CDs surface [34,35]. In addition, the absolute QY of N-CDs is approximately 27.8%, which is comparable with the reported dual-excitation and other doped CDs in Fig. 2(f) and Table S1 (Circle filled with half shadow represents the dual-excitation CDs).

Given their sensing capacity required for various environments, the luminescence stabilities of N-CDs are critical in various application scenarios. Firstly, under irradiation by UV light, the relative fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at different timescales are monitored and presented in Fig. 2(g), indicating that N-CDs have good PL stability under ultraviolet irradiation. Also, the PL intensity of N-CDs exhibits tiny attenuation under irradiation of daylight for 24 days (Fig. 2(h)). Next, the effects of salinity are investigated in various concentration of NaCl solution (0–2 mol‧L−1), showing that N-CDs process a good salt resistance. To sum up, N-CDs have an excellent fluorescence stability against the environment interference, paving a way for implementing the optosensing in various application scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of N-CDs/curcumin system for F− detection based on bimodal excitation-based ratiometric method
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of N-CDs/curcumin system as a linkage ratiometric sensor is explored. Firstly, it is observed that curcumin can be quantitatively detected by the fluorescence signals (PLE and PL) attenuation of this sensing system based on FRET (Figure S3). Notably, the fluorescence photos in Figure S4 shows that the fluorescence of N-CDs quenches efficiently under the UV lamp. Furtherly, according the presentation of Figure S5, the formation of interaction between curcumin and F− induces the blue-shift of the curcumin absorption band from 526 to 418 nm. This results in a large overlap area between the absorption spectrum of the curcumin-F− complex and the localized excitation of N-CDs, proving the existence of L-IFE and possibility for monitoring F−. Therefore, it is probable for the ratiometric sensing of F− based on the multiple excitation signals variation.

Subsequently, an interference study is carried to evaluate the selectivity performance of the proposed linkage sensing system. Various inorganic ions (Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, K+, Al3+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Na+, Ba2+, Sn2+, ClO4 −, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, CO3 2− and F−) are used as interference compounds under two different excitations. As depicted in Figure S6, only F− can induce distinct fluorescence intensity changes while other inorganic ions generate inappreciable effects. In addition, the selectivity of the linkage system toward F− under 399 nm excitation is nearly in accord with that of other interferents and obviously inferior to that under 470 nm excitation. Overall, our proposed method is preeminently feasible and selective for F− detection, making it suitable for future applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimized sensing conditions of F− determination
ce:section.ce:para: In order to maximize the sensing performance for F− detection, the experimental factors, such as solvent, pH value and the optimal concentration of N-CDs and curcumin, are studied. Firstly, the solvent of the sensing system is crucial for detection efficiency because it affects the solubleness of curcumin. Considering that curcumin dissolves readily in acetone and pure solvent is inapplicable in the practical analysis, a mixture solvent of acetone and water in a ratio of 1:1 is used as the solvent. Notably, the pH level plays a significant role in the construction of the linkage system. In Figure S7, the N-CDs/curcumin system appears yellow in the pH range from 2 to 7 while becoming orange at pH 8 and 10, and change to pink while the color of solution returns to yellow after the cascade addition of F−. Thus, the pH value is selected at 9 to satisfy the requirement of the linkage system for F− determination due to L-IFE. Additionally, N-CDs concentration is essentially associated with the distance between its excitation peaks because of the specificity of N-CDs photoluminescence. Thus, the optimal concentration of N-CDs is selected to be 1 mg‧mL−1 so that two excitation peaks are separated by a suitable distance (Figure S8). Besides, the fluorescence recovery efficiency of N-CDs/curcumin system is closely associated with the curcumin concentration and incubation time. As shown in Figure S9, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs significantly decreases when exposed to curcumin. Upon the cascade addition of F−, its corresponding fluorescence recovers and reaches the highest value when the curcumin concentration is 60 µM under two different excitations. On this basis, Figure S10 displays that the fluorescence recovering of the quenched N-CDs/curcumin system happens accompanied by increasing incubation time, and then completes in 180 s after the addition of F−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bimodal excitation-based ratiometric sensing of F−
ce:section.ce:para: Under the above optimal experiment conditions, F− with various concentrations are introduced to the linkage detection system to evaluate its sensing performance. Fig. 3 (a) shows a remarkable fluorescence recovering of the mixture under 470 nm excitation, whereas the fluorescence remains weak under 399 nm excitation. It is observed that the excitation intensity ratio of I470/I399 (I399 and I470 denote the intensities of N-CDs at the excitation wavelengths of 399 nm and 470 nm, respectively) increases with the concentration of F− ranging from 0 to 265 μM (Fig. 3(b)). And the linear regression equation (Fig. 3(c)) is I470/I399 = 0.0107C F −+1.3356 (C F − is the concentration of F−), with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9799. The detection limit is calculated to be 57 nM (well below the maximum allowable levels of 52.6 μM in drinking water) based on the equation LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the corrected blank signals of the N-CDs/curcumin and s is the slope of the calibration curve. Correspondingly, an apparent fluorescence off–on switching change can be visually perceived by the naked eyes in Figure S11. Thus, the visual recognition ability of N-CDs/curcumin system is superior to that of the mono signal probe in visual detection, and our method offers a comparable or superior linear range and LOD compared with other methods (Table S2). More significantly, such an entire detection implies that the N-CDs/curcumin system can be used as a new kind of F− sensing probe, with a straightforward operation process and great potentiality for the detection of F− in practical samples.

Subsequently, to evaluate the practical applicability of the sensing system designed in this study, the N-CDs/curcumin system is used for the assay of F− in real samples (tap water, milk and lake water). Simultaneously, the HPLC analysis is used as a contradistinction strategy of F− detection. It is listed in Table S3 that the recoveries are within the rational range of 103.8–109.4%, and the corresponding relative standard deviation (RSD) are both below 3.26% for both fluorescence and HPLC methods. Moreover, the t-test analysis reveals that the calculated t-values does not exceed the critical values (2.78), showing that there are no significant differences between both methods [17]. Hence, these results confirm good precision and accuracy of our newly presented method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the N-CDs/curcumin system response to F−
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the response mechanism of the linkage system, the interaction between the N-CDs/curcumin system and F− is investigated in detail. As seen from Fig. 3(d), the 3D fluorescence map of N-CDs exhibits two primary peaks due to the bimodal excitation behavior, contributing to the construction of a bimodal excitation-based ratiometric sensing system. After the addition of curcumin, the emission at 559 nm and the bimodal excitation at 399 nm and 470 nm of N-CDs quench efficaciously (Fig. 3(e)) because of a notable overlap area between the UV–vis absorption of curcumin and the emission of N-CDs. In addition, as displayed in Figure S12, the UV–vis absorption spectrum upon interaction of N-CDs and curcumin completely overlaps with the physical addition of the two (the theoretical), which means that there is no complex formation in the quenching process. It is supposed that the fluorescence quenching is mainly resulted from FRET [36]. Meaningfully, when F− ions are introduced in the N-CDs/curcumin system, F− binds with the curcumin to form the curcumin-F− coordination compound because of the extremely strong hydrogen bonds. Simultaneously, the existence of F− affects electronic properties of chromophore, bringing a vivid color change of curcumin-F− complex (Figure S7). On the other hand, the blue-shift of the absorption band from the interaction of curcumin and F−, intercepts the FRET between N-CDs and curcumin and merely overlaps with the short-wavelength excitation of N-CDs, leading to the occurrence of L-IFE [37]. In this way, the intensity of N-CDs at PLE = 399 nm is slightly recovered; by contrast, the intensity of N-CDs at PLE = 470 nm is remarkably improved (Fig. 3(f)). Moreover, the curcumin-F− complex merely enhances the intensity of the absorption band at 400–450 nm without notable change, which is nearly consistent with the physical addition of the absorption spectra of N-CDs and curcumin-F− complex, proving no interaction between them (Fig. 3(g)). Therefore, we propose that L-IFE is the predominant response mechanism during this different fluorescence recovering process. To further verify this assumption, the lifetime decay profiles of N-CDs, N-CDs/curcumin and N-CDs/curcumin-F− are measured and results are listed in Table S4. The fluorescence lifetime values of N-CDs at the excitations of 399 nm and 470 nm are calculated to be approximately 3.12 ns and 3.14 ns with the addition of curcumin, respectively, confirming that curcumin has a dramatic effect on the PL decay pathway of N-CDs (Fig. 3(h)). It demonstrates the high FRET efficient between curcumin and N-CDs. Furtherly, the small differences in lifetimes of N-CDs and N-CDs/curcumin-F− solutions illustrate the fluorescence response is caused by IFE (Fig. 3(i)),[38] however, the fluorescence recovery intensity of N-CDs at PL = 559 nm at an excitation of 470 nm is distinguished from that under the excitation of 399 nm in the presence of curcumin-F−. Accordingly, we infer that the fluorescence response of N-CDs toward curcumin-F− is IFE but with the locality. Therefore, these results imply that L-IFE is responsible for the fluorescence quenching and recovering at 399 nm and 470 nm, respectively. The intrinsic response mechanism diagram is shown in Fig. 3(j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-integrated sensing platform for visual and real-time determination of F−
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, due to the superior digital image colorimetry in identifying color and brightness changes, smartphone is evolving as competent analytical devices, and makes the detection more convenient, digitized and miniaturized [39]. Thus, a portable smartphone-assisted sensing platform is constructed for quantitative F− analysis. This smartphone-assisted sensing device is a portable dark acrylic box equipped with a detachable push–pull drawer, a mobile phone and two sample tube slots (which can attach two different wavelength light sources). In addition, aimed at minimizing the cost of the portable device and ensuring the detection performance, the 365 nm excitation source is selected as the short wavelength light source compared with the rare and expensive 399 nm excitation source. For the RGB analysis of the images, the application called “Color Picker” has the ability to do content calculation. According to the strategy for F− detection based on smartphone imaging colorimetry, Fig. 4 (a) illustrates the entire sensing process using the N-CDs/curcumin system. After the interaction of the N-CDs/curcumin system with various concentrations of F−, the color and brightness of the solutions show a variation differentially at excitations of 365 nm and 470 nm. Subsequently, the photo of the mixture is captured using the camera in the smartphone. Finally, RGB signal values of the photo are read from the smartphone software “Color Picker”, and entered into the WPS Office to obtain a linear relationship of RGB values and F− concentration. As displayed in Fig. 4(b), with the quantitative addition of F− (0 µM, 15 µM, 60 µM and 80 µM) into the N-CDs/curcumin system, the distinct fluorescence changes are observed under two different wavelength excitations. To further improve the accuracy and anti-interference of the imaging colorimetry, the S2/S1 and F− concentration are chosen (S1 and S2 represent the total values of RGB at excitations of 365 nm and 470 nm, respectively) as the vertical and horizontal coordinate parameters respectively to set up an internal relationship (Fig. 4(c), S2/S1 = 0.0117C F −+1.0451, C F − is the concentration of F−). It can minimize the interference of the ambience during the shooting process (Figure S13). The S2/S1 increases linearly with the increase of the F- concentration in the range of 0–60 µM with a low LOD of 0.15 µM (R2 = 0.9897), which is comparable with that measured by fluorescence instruments.

Furthermore, this portable smartphone-integrated sensing platform is also employed in real samples to testify its practical applicability. Thereinto, tap water, milk and lake water are selected as practical samples to assess the performance of the above platform by spiking recovery experiments, as depicted in Fig. 4(d). The execution of the test flow is shown in Video S1, with the recoveries of 102.64–103.72% and detection results of 51.32–51.86 μM obtained in Table S5. All above results confirm that this proposed bimodal excitation-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing method holds great potential for sensitive and visual sensing of F− in realistic environments and food.

In conclusion, we develop an innovative linkage fluorescence detection method for F− based on the bimodal excitation behavior of N-CDs. According to the binding of F− to curcumin, the N-CDs/curcumin sensing system is constructed, exhibiting a highly sensitive response to F−, with a wide linear range (0–50 µM) and a low LOD (57 nM). The detection mechanism can be interpreted as the L-IFE. Namely, the short-wavelength excitation fluorescence maintains the feeble state, and the long-wavelength excitation fluorescence recovers when the N-CDs/curcumin system exposes to F−. Moreover, the N-CDs/curcumin system shows the apparently differential changes of fluorescence under two different excitations, which lays a foundation for constructing a smartphone-integrated sensing device for the visual sensing of F−. Noteworthy to mention that, this bimodal excitation-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing system is first reported for smartphone-integrated visual sensing of F−. Further, this well-designed sensing device is successfully employed for F− determination in actual water and food samples with favorable recoveries. Our work will open a unique route for designs of ratiometric probes with fluorescence excitation characteristics as signal, as well as showcasing a promising visual application in the field of food monitoring.

Guoqiang Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Tingting Li: Software, Data curation. Yiru Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Lintao Li: Data curation. Siyuan Tang: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Houwen Hu: Data curation. Hai Jiang: Supervision, Validation. Da Chen: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108991.
